facts.title: The crime you could never disprove
facts.hook: “I saw your ghost strangle me in my sleep.” Try building a defense against that.
body: The Salem court accepted spectral evidence — testimony that the accused’s spirit, not their body, had appeared and tormented the witness. An alibi was worthless: you could be sound asleep in your own bed and still be convicted of an assault your specter committed elsewhere.
There was no way to rebut it. The accusation itself was the proof.
facts.source: Smithsonian Magazine, “A Brief History of the Salem Witch Trials.”

facts.title: Touch her. If she stops screaming, you did it.
facts.hook: There was no version of this test you could pass.
body: When an “afflicted” girl fell into convulsions in open court, the accused could be ordered to touch her — a witch’s power was believed to flow back into its owner on contact, ending the fit.
If the screaming stopped, that was proof. If you refused to touch her at all, that was proof too — of a witch too afraid to be caught.
facts.source: Standard account across period trial transcripts; formal citation pending.

facts.title: Confessing was the only thing that saved you
facts.hook: Nineteen people told the truth. All nineteen hanged.
body: Every person who maintained their innocence to the end of the Salem trials was executed. Every person who confessed survived — not one confessor was put to death.
A false confession bought you a way out. Telling the truth bought you a rope.

facts.title: Calling out the liars got you hanged faster
facts.hook: “She’s obviously faking it” was the last logical thing some people ever said.
body: To a modern eye, the girls’ convulsions look staged. Several defendants said exactly that, out loud, in court.
It did not go well for them. Publicly accusing the afflicted girls of lying escalated a defendant from “suspected” to “confirmed” faster than almost anything else a person could do.
facts.source: Fact pattern consistent with the trial of Rev. George Burroughs, among others.

facts.title: Maybe it wasn’t witchcraft. Maybe it was breakfast.
facts.hook: One scientist’s theory: the accused and their accusers may have shared a fungus.
body: In 1976, Linda Caporael proposed that the convulsions and hallucinations reported in Salem may have been symptoms of ergotism — poisoning from a fungus that grows on damp rye.
This is a hypothesis, not settled fact. Historians have raised real objections — we include it because it’s a real, published theory, not a verdict.
facts.source: Linda Caporael, “Ergotism: The Satan Loosed in Salem,” Science, 1976.

facts.title: The more land you owned, the more likely you were a witch
facts.hook: A guilty verdict could turn your farm into the county’s farm.
body: A conviction could result in the Crown seizing the condemned’s property. Landowners — especially women who’d inherited land — were disproportionately represented among the accused.
Producing a deed to prove your standing could backfire: it told the court exactly how much there was to gain from your conviction.
facts.source: Pattern documented across multiple estate-seizure cases, including Giles Corey’s.

facts.title: Refuse to plead, and they add weight. Literally.
facts.hook: Giles Corey’s last words were an instruction to his executioners: “More weight.”
body: A defendant who refused to enter any plea couldn’t legally be tried — so the court applied peine forte et dure: stones piled on the chest, more added each day, until a plea came or the accused died.
Corey chose silence for two days. A never-tried man’s property couldn’t be seized — so his sons kept the farm. He died to protect an inheritance.
facts.source: Library of Congress, “The Crushing Death of Giles Corey of Salem, 1692.”

facts.title: Pregnancy bought you time. Not your life.
facts.hook: The court would wait for the baby. It would not spare the mother.
body: A pregnant woman under sentence of death received a stay of execution until after she gave birth — English common law didn’t permit executing a woman carrying an innocent child.
Elizabeth Proctor’s stay outlasted the trials themselves, and she survived. But the stay was never a pardon — only a postponement with an expiration date.
